message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-001 in src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
What: _resolve_direction refuses a directionless LOCKED whole-corpus hypothesis (tail must be pre-registered; confirm-time direction is cross-check only) — closes the HARKing hole.
Test(s): test_whole_corpus_rejects_directionless_locked_hypothesis
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify fix G-001. Let me start by examining the changed file and test.

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	import warnings
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
55	        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
56	        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
57	        warnings.warn(
58	            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
59	            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
60	            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
61	            stacklevel=3,
62	        )
63	        require_locked(hypothesis)
64	
65	
66	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
67	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
68	
69	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
70	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
71	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
72	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
73	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
74	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
75	    """
76	    if not hypothesis.locked:
77	        raise FirewallViolation(
78	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
79	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
84	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
85	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
86	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
87	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
88	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
89	
90	
91	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
92	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
93	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
94	
95	
96	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
97	    if not math.isfinite(value):
98	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
99	
100	
101	def verdict_from_pvalue(
102	    hypothesis_id: str,
103	    *,
104	    statistic: float,
105	    p_value: float,
106	    alpha: float,
107	    powered: bool,
108	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
109	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
110	) -> Verdict:
111	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
112	
113	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
114	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
115	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
116	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
117	
118	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
119	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
120	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
121	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
122	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
123	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
124	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
125	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
126	    """
127	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
128	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
129	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
130	
131	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
132	    common = dict(
133	        statistic=statistic,
134	        threshold=alpha,
135	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
136	    )
137	    if not powered:
138	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
139	    if p_value <= alpha:
140	        if direction_supports_claim:
141	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
142	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
143	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
144	
145	
146	def confirm_unit_level(
147	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
148	    *,
149	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
150	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
151	    statistic: float,
152	    p_value: float,
153	    alpha: float,
154	    powered: bool,
155	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
156	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
157	) -> Verdict:
158	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
159	
160	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
161	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
162	
163	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
164	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
165	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
166	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
167	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
168	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
169	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
170	    """
171	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
172	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
173	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
174	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
175	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
176	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
177	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
178	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
179	    # against an unrecognized tail.
180	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
181	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
182	    ):
183	        raise ValueError(
184	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
185	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
186	        )
187	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
188	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
189	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
190	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
191	        statistic=statistic,
192	        p_value=p_value,
193	        alpha=alpha,
194	        powered=powered,
195	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
196	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
197	    )
198	
199	
200	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
201	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
202	    n = len(null)
203	    if tail == "greater":
204	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
205	    else:
206	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
207	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
208	
209	
210	def _resample_stability(
211	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
212	) -> float:
213	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
214	
215	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
216	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
217	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
218	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
219	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
220	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
221	
222	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
223	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
224	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
225	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
226	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
227	    """
228	    n = len(null)
229	    try:
230	        import numpy as np
231	
232	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
233	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
234	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
235	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
236	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
237	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
238	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
239	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
240	        if tail == "greater":
241	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
242	        else:
243	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
244	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
245	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
246	    except ImportError:
247	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
248	        return significant / len(resamples)
249	
250	
251	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
252	
253	
254	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
255	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
256	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
257	
258	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
259	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
260	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
261	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
262	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
263	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
264	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
265	
266	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
267	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
268	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
269	    """
270	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
271	    if locked_dir is None:
272	        raise ValueError(
273	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
274	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
275	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
276	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
277	        )
278	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
279	        raise ValueError(
280	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
281	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
282	        )
283	    resolved = locked_dir
284	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
285	        raise ValueError(
286	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
287	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
288	        )
289	    return resolved
290	
291	
292	def confirm_whole_corpus(
293	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
294	    *,
295	    observed: float,
296	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
297	    alpha: float,
298	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
299	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
300	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
301	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
302	) -> Verdict:
303	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
304	
305	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
306	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
307	
308	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
309	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
310	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
311	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
312	
313	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
314	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
315	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
316	
317	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
318	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
319	    """
320	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
321	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
322	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
323	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
324	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
325	    if not null_distribution:
326	        raise ValueError(
327	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
328	        )
329	    for v in null_distribution:
330	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
331	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
332	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
333	
334	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
335	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
336	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
337	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
338	    n = len(null_distribution)
339	
340	    stability: float | None = None
341	    contra_stability: float | None = None
342	    if resample_statistics is not None:
343	        if not resample_statistics:
344	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
345	        for r in resample_statistics:
346	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
347	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
348	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
349	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
350	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
351	        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
352	            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
353	        )
354	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
355	
356	    rule = (
357	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
358	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
359	    )
360	    evidence = {
361	        "p_support": p_support,
362	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
363	        "null_n": n,
364	        "predicted_direction": direction,
365	        "stability": stability,
366	        "contradiction_stability": contra_stability,
367	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
368	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
369	    }
370	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
371	
372	    # A contradiction (significant in the OPPOSITE tail) is reportable without a stability gate, and
373	    # this asymmetry with `supported` is INTENTIONAL (#G-005): support is a positive claim that must
374	    # reproduce to be decisive, whereas a statistic landing significantly in the wrong tail is
375	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
376	    # `contradiction_stability` is recorded in evidence (when resamples were supplied) so an auditor
377	    # can see whether the contradiction also reproduces, even though it is not gated on it.
378	    if p_contra <= alpha:
379	        return Verdict.contradicted(
380	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
381	        )
382	    if stability is None:
383	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
384	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
385	            rule,
386	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
387	            **common,
388	        )
389	    if not stable:
390	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
391	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
392	        )
393	    if p_support <= alpha:
394	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
395	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
396	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
397	    )
398

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -10 && echo "---DIFF---" && git log -p -3 -- src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py | head -200
input.description: Show recent commits and diff history of confirm.py

content.content:
7dccfd8 fix(hardening-p4): batch E — egress, error contracts, PII & test discrimination (8 findings)

commit 2eea9584d8151b0893d19cb580c69508492870a9
Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 23:56:24 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch C — provenance, observability & resample memory (G-009,010,012,023)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed findings (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated where applicable):
    
    - G-009 (medium) run_study now persists the hash-chained provenance trail as a JSON
      artifact and correlates it to the MLflow run (tracker.log_artifact), so an auditor
      opening a run can retrieve the provenance behind its metrics. Best-effort, like the
      other tracker calls.
    - G-010 (medium) the numpy resample-stability path uses searchsorted on a sorted null
      (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) instead of an R×N boolean matrix that peaked at
      ~250MB for R=5000,N=50000 and could OOM. Integer counts are identical (the 300-case
      numpy==pure equivalence test still passes).
    - G-012 (medium) from_records raises the typed ProvenanceError (not a raw KeyError) on a
      record missing a required field, so all trail-deserialization failures surface as one
      catchable error.
    - G-023 (low) the corpus unit-id set is computed once and reused for the split-id and
      feature-id validations (was built twice on the same corpus).
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 509 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py b/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
index 110469f..283cdad 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
@@ -229,12 +229,18 @@ def _resample_stability(
     try:
         import numpy as np
 
-        null_arr = np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64)
+        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
+        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
+        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
+        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
+        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
+        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
+        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
         res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
         if tail == "greater":
-            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
+            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
         else:
-            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] <= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
+            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
         p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
         return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
     except ImportError:

commit d61bd4e94e455584b216d5b121d026dc0373c16c
Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 23:45:16 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch A — methodology integrity (G-001,005,006,011,017)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed findings (2-agent verified after adversarial self-refutation;
    revert-discriminated tests):
    
    - G-001 (high) close the HARKing hole: a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis could be locked with
      predicted_direction=None (binding "no direction") and then have its tail chosen at
      confirm time after seeing the data, flipping supported<->contradicted. _resolve_direction
      now REFUSES a directionless locked hypothesis — the tail must be pre-registered; a
      confirm-time direction is a cross-check only, never a supply. (closes the gap #24 claimed)
    - G-005 (medium) the contradiction/support stability asymmetry is now intentional + DOC'd
      (a contradiction is evidence OF the opposite, not absence of evidence, so it is not gated
      on stability) AND the opposite-tail stability is recorded in evidence
      (contradiction_stability) so an auditor sees whether it reproduces.
    - G-006 (medium) confirm primitives warn when authority=None (presence-only gate verifies
      the proof by presence, not cryptographically) — a direct caller can no longer silently
      skip real pre-registration.
    - G-011 (medium) discover_and_confirm type-guards the confirmer's return (non-Verdict ->
      FirewallViolation), matching the pipeline/adjudication guards from #F-021.
    - G-017 (low) documented that the unit-level verdict is governed by the caller flag, not
      the locked predicted_direction (advisory at unit level).
    
    Self-refutation pre-killed 7 assessment findings (incl. re-flags of the pass-3 _release
    idempotency fix and an OTLP-endpoint override, both unreachable).
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 505 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py b/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
index fbf9ad4..110469f 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
@@ -25,6 +25,7 @@ from __future__ import annotations
 
 import datetime as _dt
 import math
+import warnings
 from typing import Iterable, Sequence
 
 from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
@@ -50,6 +51,15 @@ def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority
             hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
         )
     else:
+        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
+        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
+        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
+        warnings.warn(
+            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
+            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
+            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
+            stacklevel=3,
+        )
         require_locked(hypothesis)
 
 
@@ -149,6 +159,14 @@ def confirm_unit_level(
 
     Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
     lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
+
+    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
+    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
+    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
+    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
+    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
+    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
+    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
     """
     if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
         raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
@@ -228,28 +246,35 @@ _VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
 
 
 def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
-    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
-    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one.
-
-    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}`` and any other value is
-    rejected: an unrecognized direction would otherwise fall through to the 'less' tail in
-    :func:`_empirical_p`, silently flipping supported↔contradicted (audit pass 2, #128).
+    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
+    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
+
+    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
+    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
+    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
+    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
+    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
+    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
+    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
+
+    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
+    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
+    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
     """
     locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
-    if locked_dir is not None:
-        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
-            raise ValueError(
-                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
-                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
-            )
-        resolved = locked_dir
-    elif predicted_direction is None:
+    if locked_dir is None:
         raise ValueError(
-            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
-            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
+            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
+            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
+            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
+            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
         )
-    else:
-        resolved = predicted_direction
+    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
+        raise ValueError(
+            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
+            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
+        )
+    resolved = locked_dir
     if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
         raise ValueError(
             f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
@@ -307,12 +332,19 @@ def confirm_whole_corpus(
     n = len(null_distribution)
 
     stability: float | None = None
+    contra_stability: float | None = None
     if resample_statistics is not None:
         if not resample_statistics:
             raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
         for r in resample_statistics:
             _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
         stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
+        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
+        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
+        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
+        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
+            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
+        )

Now let me verify discrimination empirically: revert the fix and confirm the test fails, then restore. The key discriminating case is the second one (confirm-time direction supplied) — under the old code that path would set `resolved=predicted_direction` and proceed without raising.

Let me run the test on current code, then simulate the revert.

content.text: Passes on current code. Now verify discrimination by reverting `_resolve_direction` to the old logic in-place and re-running.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cp src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py /tmp/confirm_backup.py && cat > /tmp/old_resolve.py << 'EOF'
import re
f = "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py"
s = open(f).read()
# locate function body and replace logic block
old = '''    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
    if locked_dir is None:
        raise ValueError(
            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
        )
    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
        raise ValueError(
            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
        )
    resolved = locked_dir'''
new = '''    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
    if locked_dir is not None:
        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
            raise ValueError("conflict")
        resolved = locked_dir
    elif predicted_direction is None:
        raise ValueError("no predicted_direction")
    else:
        resolved = predicted_direction'''
assert old in s
open(f,"w").write(s.replace(old,new))
print("reverted")
EOF
.venv/bin/python /tmp/old_resolve.py 2>/dev/null || python3 /tmp/old_resolve.py

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_confirm.py::test_whole_corpus_rejects_directionless_locked_hypothesis -q 2>&1 | tail -20; echo "=== restoring ==="; cp /tmp/confirm_backup.py src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py; git diff --stat

content.text:
The test discriminates. With the old logic reverted, the test FAILS. Critically, the second assertion (confirm-time direction supplied) is the real HARKing-hole closer — under old code that path returns `resolved=predicted_direction` and produces a verdict with no error at all. The `match="not pre-registered"` also pins the new behavior precisely.

Let me confirm the file is fully restored and the broader suite is green.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git diff --stat && echo "=== full confirm suite ===" && .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_confirm.py -q 2>&1 | tail -5
input.description: Verify clean restore and run full confirm test suite

content.text:
File is restored (clean diff), and all 40 confirm tests pass.

Assessment:

Appropriateness — The fix in `_resolve_direction` (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:254-289) is correct and idiomatic. A hypothesis reaching `_resolve_direction` has already passed `_gate_preregistration`, so it is locked and its `predicted_direction` is part of the cryptographic binding. A `None` locked direction therefore genuinely attests "no tail pre-registered"; the new code refuses it outright rather than letting a confirm-time argument supply the tail post-hoc. The confirm-time argument is correctly demoted to cross-check-only (matches the locked dir or errors). The downstream `_VALID_DIRECTIONS` guard (#128) is preserved. No regression: full confirm suite passes, including the existing direction-conflict and unit-level advisory-direction tests.

Completeness — The test discriminates. Reverting to the old branch logic makes it FAIL (raises "no predicted_direction" instead of "not pre-registered"). The test covers both the directionless-no-arg path AND the load-bearing HARKing path (confirm-time direction supplied) — under old code the latter silently produced a verdict with no error, which is exactly the hole G-001 describes. The `match="not pre-registered"` pin ties the assertion to the new contract.

input.reasoning:
Fix is correct, idiomatic, and regression-free; test discriminates (fails on revert).

APPROPRIATE: In src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:254-289, _resolve_direction now refuses a directionless LOCKED whole-corpus hypothesis. The reasoning is sound: by the time _resolve_direction runs, _gate_preregistration has already passed, so the hypothesis is locked and its predicted_direction is part of the binding. A None locked direction thus attests "no tail pre-registered"; previously the code accepted a confirm-time predicted_direction to SUPPLY the tail (old branch: `else: resolved = predicted_direction`), letting the analyst pick the tail after seeing observed data and flip supported<->contradicted (HARKing). New code raises ValueError outright when locked_dir is None, and demotes the confirm-time arg to cross-check-only (must match locked dir or error). The _VALID_DIRECTIONS guard (#128) is preserved. No regression: full tests/test_confirm.py suite (40 tests) passes.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: I empirically reverted _resolve_direction to the old branch logic in place; test_whole_corpus_rejects_directionless_locked_hypothesis FAILED (got "no predicted_direction" / no error on the confirm-time path instead of "not pre-registered"). Restored, it passes. The test has two assertions: (1) directionless lock with no confirm-time arg, and (2) the load-bearing HARKing path — directionless lock WITH predicted_direction="less" supplied at confirm time, which under old code returned resolved=predicted_direction and produced a verdict with NO error at all. The match="not pre-registered" pins the new contract. File restored clean (git diff empty).

content.text: Verified G-001 as CONFIRMED. The fix correctly closes the HARKing hole and the named regression test discriminates (it fails when the fix is reverted).
